results.possibleIdentity: Fred J. Kamphoefner (1921–2003) was a prominent electrical engineer at the Stanford Research Institute (SRI), where he played a critical role in several major technological advancements. He is best known for being a key member of the team that developed the Electronic Recording Machine, Accounting (ERMA) system and Magnetic Ink Character Recognition (MICR) technology in the 1950s, which revolutionized automated banking. He graduated from the University of California, Berkeley with a B.S. in Electrical Engineering in 1943. During World War II, he worked at the Radio Research Laboratory at Harvard University on radar countermeasures before following Fred Terman to Stanford. At SRI, Kamphoefner held several leadership roles, including Manager of the Physical Electronics Laboratory in the 1960s and Director of the Engineering Sciences Laboratory by the late 1970s. His technical expertise extended to automatic character recognition and early ink-jet printing technology, notably authoring a significant paper on the subject in the IEEE Transactions on Electron Devices in 1972. In 2001, he was honored with SRI's Weldon B. Gibson Achievement Award for his contributions to society. His professional activity during the late 1960s and early 1970s at SRI aligns with the period when many technical professionals were recruited for collaborations through Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.).
